title: Nonreversible Gauge Fields in Fokker--Planck Dynamics: Supersymmetric Hamiltonians and Learned Finite Forces
abstract: We formulate stationary-density-preserving nonreversible perturbations of Fokker--Planck dynamics as gauge fields that deform relaxation spectra while leaving the invariant state fixed. When detailed balance holds, a similarity transformation maps the reversible Fokker--Planck operator to a Witten-Laplacian-type supersymmetric Hamiltonian; nonreversible gauges then appear as non-Hermitian perturbations that preserve the zero mode but modify the excited spectrum. This operator viewpoint gives a common language for relaxation gaps, circulating probability currents, hypocoercive acceleration, and finite control costs. We represent admissible gauge currents by antisymmetric tensor fields and identify the detailed-balance-violating Ohzeki--Ichiki force as a constant symplectic example whose infinite-strength limit is Hamiltonian dynamics. The continuous-time spectral gap alone does not select a finite gauge strength, so we introduce a finite-time regularized objective and an actor--critic procedure for learning the gauge. An exactly solvable anisotropic Gaussian Ornstein--Uhlenbeck benchmark separates the spectral transition from the finite-time optimum and shows that the learned gauge recovers the Lyapunov-equation optimum. A double-well benchmark then illustrates the same constrained selection in a nonconvex metastable landscape. Stochastic gradient methods enter this framework as physically relevant Fokker--Planck systems: mini-batch noise acts as an effective diffusion tensor, and adaptive methods such as Adam correspond to metric choices with possible nonequilibrium currents.
summary: This paper unifies the study of stochastic processes by treating nonreversible perturbations as gauge fields within the Fokker-Planck framework. It introduces an optimal control method using Actor-Critic techniques to learn the best nonreversible force that accelerates sampling without altering the true equilibrium distribution. This has significant implications for understanding advanced MCMC and optimization algorithms.
main_problem: The work develops an operator-theoretic framework for designing nonreversible perturbations to Fokker-Planck dynamics while ensuring the stationary probability density remains unchanged.
main_result: The authors show that optimal control of relaxation spectra can be achieved by learning a gauge field strength, which balances finite-time convergence speed against the cost of the applied nonreversible force.
method: The problem is formulated as an optimal control problem solved using Actor-Critic methods, minimizing a regularized objective function derived from simulating forward Fokker-Planck dynamics under candidate gauge fields.
model_or_system: The system models stochastic processes governed by Fokker-Planck equations, applied to optimization algorithms like Stochastic Gradient Langevin Dynamics (SGLD). The dynamics are analyzed using supersymmetric Hamiltonian formulations and nonreversible gauge potentials.
key_observables: Spectral gap ($\Delta$), stationary current ($J_{\pi}$), finite-time relaxation error ($\mathbb{E}[f_a(	heta)] - \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[f_a(	heta)]]$), and the learned gauge strength ($\gamma$ or $a^*_\lambda$).
important_parameters: The regularization parameter $\lambda$, the time horizon $	au$, and the gauge field parameters ($\psi$ or $\gamma$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on approximations like matching conditional moments to an Itô SDE, and the selection of a finite-time objective function is necessary because spectral gap alone is insufficient.
figures_summary: Figures compare trajectories in Gaussian benchmarks (showing chiral acceleration) and analyze relaxation in double-well potentials, contrasting reversible dynamics with those driven by learned nonreversible gauges.
paper_structure: The paper builds from the general operator view of stationary-density-preserving perturbations as gauge fields, develops the optimal control objective using Actor-Critic methods, and validates this framework on anisotropic Gaussian and double-well benchmarks.
why_interesting: This work provides a powerful mathematical language (gauge theory) to analyze non-equilibrium dynamics in ML optimization, connecting concepts from quantum Hamiltonians/supersymmetry to practical stochastic gradient methods.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
